digest.intro: AI cracks a 50-year conjecture, Apple sues OpenAI, and the world holds its breath.

items.title: NATO Ankara summit: Trump promises Zelensky a Patriot manufacturing license
items.blurb: A mid-year synthesis covering the NATO summit commitments, the Starmer resignation in the UK, Iran aftermath, US-China dynamics, and tariff trajectories — all in one document. The Patriot manufacturing license for Ukraine, if it holds, is a significant escalation in Western arms-transfer doctrine. Useful H2 orientation map.

items.title: GPT-5.6 Sol Ultra claims a proof of the Cycle Double Cover Conjecture
items.blurb: OpenAI is claiming a 3-page AI-generated proof of the Cycle Double Cover Conjecture — an open problem in graph theory since the 1970s — produced in under an hour using 64 coordinated subagents. Whether it survives expert scrutiny is the open question; the primary document is three pages and readable now, before the discourse catches up. This is either a historic result or the most sophisticated-looking hallucination in mathematics — worth watching closely.

items.title: The exact prompt that orchestrated 64 subagents to do original mathematics
items.blurb: OpenAI also published the full prompt used — which means you can read the design document for how a multi-agent system was instructed to attempt an unsolved conjecture. The process architecture is arguably more interesting than the proof itself: how you decompose a research task across 64 parallel agents is a new kind of systems design. This is what the 'programming' of mathematical discovery looks like in 2026.

items.title: Apple sues OpenAI over trade secret theft — the actual complaint
items.blurb: Apple is alleging that Tang Tan (former VP of product design, iPhone and Apple Watch) and Chang Liu (8-year Apple senior systems electrical engineer who joined OpenAI in January 2026) walked confidential IP on unreleased technologies directly into OpenAI — which is also building hardware with Jony Ive's io Products. The complaint names OpenAI and io Products as defendants. The irony: Apple and OpenAI announced a partnership last year. The court filing is the thing to read, not the coverage.

sections.heading: How tools think (and how we think about tools)

items.title: Good Tools Are Invisible
items.blurb: A clean, sharp essay making the case that the correct criterion for a tool is invisibility — flow state, not puzzle-solving. The specific target is the vim-as-identity phenomenon: treating a tool's friction as the point, marketing limitations as features. The author's test is blunt: if someone can do the same task in Sublime with multiple cursors in a minute while you're still building a macro, the macro isn't the win you think it is. Useful provocation for anyone who makes things that other people use.

items.title: Clay Extruder Enables Printable Pottery
items.blurb: Joshua Bird built a ceramic 3D printer from scratch, solving the clay rheology problem (soft enough to flow, stiff enough to bridge) by pressurizing the clay feed as much as possible — which lets you use stiffer mixtures. The killer detail: every plastic auger he 3D-printed was abraded to nothing by the clay in under an hour; only a stainless steel printed auger survived. The result is a working chain-mail mesh fired in ceramic, which is a sentence that shouldn't exist.

items.title: Gil Batle's double life encoded in porcelain
items.blurb: Gil Batle spent 25 years incarcerated in California prisons, teaching himself to draw and eventually tattooing clandestinely. Now living in the Philippines, he encodes those memories into white porcelain plates alongside Hiligaynon folk flora — bird cages, chains, shivs, and barbs nested inside the most genteel domestic object imaginable. The structural tension is the whole point: the fragility and implied civility of porcelain holding scenes of brutality. Show at Ricco/Maresca in New York through August 21.

facts.fact: The Cycle Double Cover Conjecture — that every bridgeless graph has a collection of cycles covering each edge exactly twice — has been open since the 1970s, and OpenAI claims a 3-page proof was generated in under an hour by 64 coordinated AI subagents.

Tang Tan, one of the defendants in Apple's lawsuit against OpenAI, was VP of product design at Apple leading iPhone and Apple Watch design before departing in February 2024 — ultimately to work on hardware with Jony Ive's io Products, which is also named as a defendant.

New York City's new subscription rule, starting October 1, fines companies $525 per user subscription for failing to provide a simple cancellation path — making NYC the first US city to implement such a ban.

Every 3D-printed plastic auger Joshua Bird used in his ceramic printer was abraded to destruction by clay in under an hour — only a 3D-printed stainless steel auger survived long enough to successfully print a fired chain-mail mesh.

The first human Phase I trials of partial cell reprogramming — attempting to rejuvenate cells without triggering full pluripotency and tumor risk — began in April 2026, using a 56-day protocol targeting age-related eye disease.

art_picks.title: Tetsuo: The Iron Man
art_picks.creator: Shinya Tsukamoto
art_picks.why: A film that is essentially a WebGL shader made with human bodies — industrial noise, cybernetic body horror, and feedback loops rendered in frantic 16mm. It treats the boundary between flesh and machine as something that collapses rather than a line to cross, which should speak directly to someone working at the physical/digital edge.

art_picks.title: Coded Environments (ongoing web works)
art_picks.creator: Casey Reas
art_picks.why: Reas writes software as a drawing practice — his process-based pieces generate forms that feel both organic and rigidly governed by rule systems, making the code itself the brushstroke. The work sits at exactly the intersection of generative 3D aesthetics and systems-thinking that Willem builds in.

art_picks.title: Music Has the Right to Children
art_picks.creator: Boards of Canada
art_picks.why: The record functions like a found-footage reel of a childhood that never happened — built from degraded tape, field recordings, and half-remembered melodies that feel like memory itself decaying. Its emotional density comes entirely from texture and structure, with no lyrics to explain itself, which is the music equivalent of generative work that lands in the gut.

events.name: Carol Bove: Survey Exhibition
events.venue: Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum, 1071 Fifth Ave, Manhattan
events.why: A career survey filling the Guggenheim rotunda — paperback book assemblages to towering steel sculptures — from someone whose practice is obsessively about how objects hold cultural memory and how structure becomes meaning; closing July 2, so this weekend is effectively the last chance.

events.name: Whitney Biennial 2026
events.venue: Whitney Museum of American Art, 99 Gansevoort St, Manhattan
events.why: 56 artists across interspecies kinships, technological affinities, and shared mythologies — curated without a thesis, shaped by 300 studio visits, and deliberately prioritizing mood and texture over argument; closing in six weeks so this is the window.

events.name: David Byrne / Saul Steinberg: Influence and Affinity
events.why: Two artists who treat drawing as a system for understanding how culture works — Steinberg's visual essays about signs and symbols in dialogue with Byrne's obsessive image-making; a rare pairing of people who went unreasonably deep on how perception and performance shape identity.

events.name: Gabriel Orozco: Public Nature
events.why: 12 large-format trompe l'oeil photographs on 300 bus shelters — vines through pipes, entropy in the built environment — designed to dissolve the boundary between image and site so each photo extends into the actual street around it; the whole city is the gallery.

events.name: SummerStage: Mavis Staples / Brother Wallace
events.why: Free outdoor show with Mavis Staples, whose voice carries sixty years of American gospel, soul, and protest music — not a nostalgia act but a living transmission of a tradition that was always about collective body and collective feeling, which is exactly the kind of thing that makes a maker want to make something.
